content:
# Leviticus Chapter 5 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59v–60r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_005_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_005_page_002.jpg`; **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 89–90 (LXX) | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_005_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_005_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And if a person [nefesh] sins in that he hears [ve-shama] the voice of an oath [qol alah], and he is a witness [ed] — whether he has seen or known — if he does not disclose [it] [ve-lo yaggid], then he shall bear his iniquity [ve-nasa avono].

**2** Or a person who touches any unclean thing [davar tame] — whether it is the carcass [nevelah] of an unclean wild animal [chayah teme'ah], or the carcass of unclean livestock [behemah teme'ah], or the carcass of unclean swarming things [sheretz tame] — and it is hidden from him [ne'lam mimmennu], and he is unclean, and he incurs guilt [ve-ashem];

**3** or when he touches human uncleanness [tum'at adam] — concerning any of his uncleanness by which he becomes unclean — and it is hidden from him, and he comes to know it, and he incurs guilt;

**4** or a person who swears [yishava] uttering with his lips [le-vatte sefatayim] rashly [le-vatte] — whether to do evil or to do good — concerning all that the human being utters rashly with an oath [shevuah], and it is hidden from him, and he comes to know it, and he incurs guilt in one of these —

**5** and it shall be, when he incurs guilt in one of these [things], then he shall confess [ve-hitvaddah] that in which he has sinned;

**6** and he shall bring his guilt payment [ashamah] to the LORD for his sin that he sinned: a female [neqevah] from the flock — a lamb [kivshah] or a female goat [se'irat izzim] — as a sin offering [le-chattat]; and the priest shall make atonement [ve-khipper] for him from his sin.

**7** And if his hand cannot reach enough [ve-lo-taggiya yado] for a lamb [se] — then he shall bring as his guilt payment [ashamam] to the LORD for that which he sinned: two turtledoves [shtei torim] or two young pigeons [shnei benei yonah] — one for a sin offering [le-chattat] and one for a burnt offering [le-olah].

**8** And he shall bring them to the priest, and he shall offer first the one that is for the sin offering; and he shall pinch its head [ve-malaq et-rosho] from its neck [mi-mul orpo] but shall not sever it [ve-lo yavdil].

**9** And he shall sprinkle [ve-hizzah] some of the blood of the sin offering upon the wall of the altar [al-qir ha-mizbeach], and the remaining blood [ha-notar ba-dam] shall be drained [yimmatsseh] at the base of the altar — it is a sin offering.

**10** And the second he shall make as a burnt offering according to the prescribed manner [ka-mishpat]; and the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin that he sinned, and it shall be forgiven [ve-nislach] him.

**11** And if his hand cannot reach enough for two turtledoves or two young pigeons, then he shall bring as his offering for what he sinned: one-tenth of an ephah [asaron] of fine flour [solet] as a sin offering; he shall not put oil [shemen] upon it and he shall not place incense [levonah] upon it, for it is a sin offering.

**12** And he shall bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take his handful [qomtso] from it as its representative portion [azkaratah] and burn [ve-hiqtir] it upon the altar, upon the fire offerings [ishei] of the LORD — it is a sin offering.

**13** And the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin that he sinned in one of these [things], and it shall be forgiven him; and it [the remainder] shall belong to the priest, like the grain offering [ke-minchah].'"

**14** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**15** "A person [nefesh] who commits unfaithfulness [ki-yim'ol ma'al] and sins unintentionally [bi-shgagah] among the holy things [be-qodshe] of the LORD — he shall bring his guilt offering [ashamah] to the LORD: a ram [ayil] without blemish from the flock, assessed by you [be-erkekha] in silver shekels, by the shekel of the sanctuary [shekel ha-qodesh], as a guilt offering [le-asham].

**16** And what he has sinned against the holy thing he shall make restitution [ve-shillem] for, and he shall add a fifth [chamishitho] to it [ve-yosef alav] and give it to the priest; and the priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt offering, and it shall be forgiven him.

**17** And if a person sins and does any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things that are not to be done, and he did not know [ve-lo yada], then he incurs guilt and shall bear his iniquity [ve-nasa avono].

**18** And he shall bring a ram without blemish from the flock, assessed by you, as a guilt offering [le-asham] to the priest; and the priest shall make atonement for him for the error [shgagato] that he committed unintentionally, when he did not know, and it shall be forgiven him.

**19** It is a guilt offering [asham hu] — he has indeed incurred guilt [asham asham] before the LORD."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 59v–60r)

1. **v.1 — Ve-shama qol alah / "he hears the voice of an oath":** The opening case is arresting in form: it is not a typical "if a person sins by doing X" but "if a person sins in that he hears." The verb שָׁמַע (shama, "to hear") governs the entire clause. The phrase קוֹל אָלָה (qol alah, "voice of an oath / adjuration") refers to a public oath-formula issued by a judicial or community authority calling for witnesses to come forward — essentially a sworn summons to testify. אָלָה (alah) is a solemn oath that includes a self-imprecation (a curse upon oneself if one does not comply). The person has heard this summons and knows information — whether by direct sight (ra'ah) or by indirect knowledge (yada) — but nonetheless withholds testimony (lo yaggid). He is obligated to speak but refuses. The result is וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (ve-nasa avono, "he shall bear his iniquity") — a phrase expressing that the guilt adheres to him personally, carried like a load. The LXX renders ἢ γὰρ εἶδεν ἢ σύνοιδεν (ē gar eiden ē sunoiden, "for either he has seen or is aware"), precisely rendering the MT's ra'ah ... yada pair. This case has no prescribed sacrifice because the issue is wilful concealment of testimony rather than inadvertent error — the guilt is stated but its resolution in this verse is left open, serving as the topical heading for the cases that follow.

2. **v.1 — Ve-lo yaggid / "he does not disclose":** יַגִּיד (yaggid, from n-g-d, hiphil: "to tell / to make known / to disclose") is used here in the negative. The hiphil of nagad carries a sense of making something visible or known that was hidden — it is a causative of "to be in front of, conspicuous." Failure to testify when adjured is a sin precisely because the community's administration of justice depends upon witnesses willing to come forward. The ancient Near Eastern legal context assumes the witness-testimony system; suppressing known evidence undermines the entire judicial structure. The LXX renders οὐκ ἀπαγγείλῃ (ouk apanggeilē, "he does not report/announce"), preserving the disclosure/announcement nuance.

3. **vv.2-3 — Touching uncleanness and "it is hidden from him" (ne'lam mimmennu):** The second and third cases involve the inadvertent contraction of ritual uncleanness [tum'ah] through contact with (a) unclean animal carcasses or (b) human uncleanness. Three categories of animal carcasses are specified: chayah teme'ah (unclean wild animal), behemah teme'ah (unclean domestic animal), and sheretz tame (unclean swarming creature / small crawling creature). The phrase נֶעְלַם מִמֶּנּוּ (ne'lam mimmennu, "hidden from him") is the key condition: the person did not know at the time, but later becomes aware. The verb עָלַם (alam, "to be hidden / concealed") in the niphal passive describes a state of ignorance that is subsequently overcome. The uncleanness structure of these verses presupposes the clean/unclean distinctions of Leviticus 11-15 (which in the canonical text comes later but is clearly already operative in practice). The LXX renders λανθάνῃ αὐτόν (lanthanē auton, "it escapes him / is hidden from him"), a close rendering of the ne'lam idiom.

4. **v.4 — Le-vatte sefatayim / "uttering rashly with the lips":** The fourth case addresses בִּטּוּי שְׂפָתַיִם (bittui sefatayim, "utterance of the lips / rash utterance"), from the root b-t-' (or b-t-h in some analyses), meaning "to speak rashly, to utter thoughtlessly." The construction לְבַטֵּא בִּשְׂפָתַיִם (le-vatte bi-sefatayim, "to utter with the lips") denotes a verbal act — specifically, swearing an oath (shevuah) that one has not thought through. The oath may be either "to do evil or to do good" (le-hara o le-hetiv) — in either direction a rash oath creates obligation. The condition ne'lam mimmennu applies here too: the person forgot about it, then later realizes he has obligated himself by an oath he cannot or has not fulfilled. Confession (v.5) and offering are then required. The LXX renders ἀποφθέγξηται τοῖς χείλεσιν (apophthenxetai tois cheileisin, "he pronounces with the lips"), using a word that connotes a bold or emphatic utterance.

5. **v.5 — Ve-hitvaddah / "he shall confess":** וְהִתְוַדָּה (ve-hitvaddah) is the reflexive/reciprocal hithpael of the root y-d-h in the hithpael stem (hitpael of yadah). In the hithpael, yadah shifts from the hiphil meaning "to give thanks / to praise" to "to confess / acknowledge." Confession (vidui) is here made a prerequisite or concomitant of the offering — the sacrifice without verbal acknowledgment of the specific sin is incomplete. This is the first appearance in Leviticus of the confession requirement. Later, at Lev 16:21 (the Day of Atonement), the high priest confesses over the live goat; in Lev 26:40 communal confession in exile is a condition for restoration. The hitpael structure ("he makes himself known regarding the sin") suggests an active, engaged acknowledgment — the offerer articulates what was done. The LXX renders ἐξαγορεύσει (exagoreusi, "he shall declare / confess publicly"), from exagoreuo, "to declare openly." This is significant: the LXX chooses a word that implies public, open declaration, not merely private acknowledgment.

6. **v.6 — Ashamah / "his guilt payment":** אֲשָׁמָה (ashamah) in this verse is the abstract noun from the root א-שׁ-מ (asham, "guilt / reparation"). In vv.6-13 it functions as a general term for the offering brought as reparation for the four cases of vv.1-4, before the technical asham (guilt offering) of vv.14-19. The ashamah here is discharged by bringing either a female lamb (kivshah) or a female goat (se'irat izzim) as a chattat (sin offering). The word ashamah thus bridges the sin offering section (vv.1-13) and the formal guilt offering section (vv.14-19): the asham root pervades the entire chapter, linking guilt-incurrence, guilt-payment, and guilt-offering into a unified semantic field. The LXX renders περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας αὐτοῦ (peri tēs hamartias autou, "concerning his sin") rather than transliterating ashamah, absorbing it into the sin offering category.

7. **v.7 — Ve-lo-taggiya yado / "his hand cannot reach enough":** וְאִם-לֹא תַגִּיעַ יָדוֹ (ve-im lo taggiya yado, literally "if his hand does not reach") is the idiom for insufficient financial means — the hand that "reaches" denotes the ability to acquire. This is the graduated offering provision: when the default animal (female lamb or goat, v.6) is beyond financial reach, two birds are permitted. The graduated scale continues in v.11: if even two birds are unaffordable, fine flour is acceptable. This progression (lamb/goat → two birds → fine flour) is one of the most striking demonstrations of the priestly system's commitment to universal access to atonement: no Israelite, however destitute, is left without the means of bringing an offering. The LXX renders ἐὰν δὲ μὴ ἱκανῷ ἡ χείρ (ean de mē hikanō hē cheir, "if the hand is not sufficient"), preserving the "sufficient hand" idiom with hikanō (sufficient/adequate).

8. **v.7 — Torim / turtledoves and benei yonah / young pigeons:** Two pairs of birds appear in the priestly offering legislation: תֹּרִים (torim, "turtledoves," singular tor) and בְּנֵי יוֹנָה (benei yonah, "sons of the dove / young pigeons"). Turtledoves are wild birds; young pigeons are domesticated. Both are small, inexpensive, and widely available. Their use across multiple Levitical contexts (burnt offering: Lev 1:14; sin offering: here; purification: Lev 12:6, 14:22, 15:14) establishes them as the standard low-cost offering animal pair. The assignment of one bird to chattat and one to olah (sin offering and burnt offering, v.7) follows the same pattern as Lev 1:14-17 and 12:6-8. The LXX renders τρυγόνας (trugones, "turtledoves") and περιστερὰς (peristeras, "pigeons/doves") throughout, both standard Greek bird names.

9. **v.8 — Ve-malaq et-rosho mi-mul orpo / "he shall pinch its head from its neck":** מָלַק (malaq, "to nip / to pinch off") describes the distinctive bird-slaughter technique used for sin offering birds (also at Lev 1:15 for burnt offering birds, though with slight variation). The head is pinched from the neck at the back (mi-mul orpo, "from opposite its nape") but not completely severed (ve-lo yavdil, "but shall not divide it"). The result is a partially detached head — the head dangles but remains connected. This contrasts with the burnt offering bird of Lev 1:15, where the head is pinched completely and burned; in the sin offering the blood manipulation takes priority. The partial neck-pinch creates a wound from which the blood is wrung out against the altar wall (v.9). The LXX renders ἀποτίλαι τὴν κεφαλήν (apotilai tēn kephalēn, "to pluck off the head"), using a word (apotilao) that connotes pulling or plucking, sometimes used for plucking feathers — here it describes the pinching action.

10. **v.9 — Al-qir ha-mizbeach / "upon the wall of the altar" and ha-notar ba-dam yimmatsseh / "the remaining blood shall be drained":** The blood manipulation for the bird sin offering is distinct from both the quadruped sin offering (vv.25-34 ch.4) and the burnt offering bird (Lev 1:15). Here the blood is הִזָּה (hizzah, "sprinkled") against the wall (qir) of the altar — the vertical face — while the remainder יִמָּצֵה (yimmatsseh, "is squeezed out / drained") at the base. The verb מָצָה (matsah) in the niphal means "to be squeezed dry / to be drained" — used elsewhere for squeezing a fleece dry (Judg 6:38) and for wringing out blood from a vessel (Lev 1:15). The full draining of remaining blood at the altar base ensures that no blood is wasted but properly deposited in the one acceptable location. The LXX renders ῥανεῖ ... στραγγιεῖ (ranei ... stranggiei, "he shall sprinkle ... shall drain drop by drop"), stranggizō being used for the wringing/draining action.

11. **v.11 — Lo-yasim aleha shemen / "he shall not put oil on it" and lo-yitten aleha levonah / "he shall not place incense on it":** The poorest person's flour offering is distinguished by two specific prohibitions: no oil and no frankincense. Both oil and incense are markers of elevation, dignity, and pleasing aroma in the priestly grain offering legislation (cf. Lev 2:1-2, where the standard minchah is accompanied by oil and frankincense). Their removal from the flour sin offering is deliberate: the offering is stripped of all adornment that might suggest celebration or festivity. It comes before the LORD in maximum austerity — raw flour, nothing more. The absence of frankincense also means the azkarah ("representative portion" or "memorial portion") that is burned will produce no pleasing incense aroma, only the smoke of plain flour. The LXX renders οὐκ ἐπιχεεῖ ἐπ᾿ αὐτὴν ἔλαιον (ouk epicheei ep' autēn elaion, "shall not pour oil upon it") and οὐ θήσει ἐπ᾿ αὐτὴν λίβανον (ou thēsei ep' autēn libanon, "shall not place frankincense upon it"), preserving both prohibitions.

12. **v.11 — Asaron / "one-tenth of an ephah":** עֶשָׂרוֹן (asaron) is a dry measure, specifically one-tenth of an ephah. An ephah is approximately 22 liters, making an asaron approximately 2.2 liters or roughly 2 quarts — a meaningful but modest quantity. The same measure is prescribed for the daily grain offering (tamid) of the priest (Lev 6:20) and for the grain component of other offerings. The asaron is the standard single-portion grain measure in the priestly system.

13. **v.12 — Azkaratah / "its representative portion":** אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah) is from the root z-k-r ("to remember / to cause to be remembered"). In the context of grain offerings, the azkarah is the handful burned on the altar — it is the "memorial portion" or "representative portion" that stands before the LORD in place of the whole. The remainder (the bulk of the flour) goes to the priest (v.13). The azkarah is not merely a token; it is the portion that "brings remembrance" of the offerer before God. This verb-root z-k-r is the same root used for God "remembering" covenants and people throughout the Torah (e.g., God remembering Noah in Gen 8:1, remembering the covenant with Abraham in Exod 2:24). The burning of the azkarah is thus an act of presenting the person's need before the divine memory. The LXX renders μνημόσυνον (mnēmosunon, "memorial / remembrance"), the standard Greek equivalent.

14. **v.13 — "Like the grain offering" (ke-minchah):** The closing clause of v.13 identifies what happens to the remaining flour: "it shall belong to the priest, like the grain offering [ke-minchah]." This is the standard provision of Lev 2:3, 10 — the priest's portion of the grain offering belongs to Aaron and his sons. The flour sin offering, despite being a chattat, ends with priestly disposition identical to the grain offering. This convergence underscores that in the graduated system the flour offering functions as both chattat (effecting atonement) and minchah (following grain offering disposition rules).

15. **v.15 — Ma'al / "unfaithfulness / trespass":** מַעַל (ma'al) is one of the most charged words in the priestly vocabulary. From the root m-'-l, it denotes a fundamental breach of faithfulness — specifically, a violation of sacred trust or a trespass against what belongs exclusively to another (here, to the LORD). Ma'al appears in contexts of misappropriating sacred property (the holy things, qodshe YHWH), of marital unfaithfulness (Num 5:12, 27), and of national apostasy (1 Chron 9:1; Neh 13:27). In Lev 5:15, the ma'al is committed "among the holy things [be-qodshe] of the LORD" — the offender has trespassed into or against what was set apart as the LORD's exclusive property. The LXX renders ἀμέλῃ ἀμέλειαν (amelē ameleian) or more precisely λάθῃ αὐτὸν λῆμμα (lathē auton lēmma) — actually the LXX uses παρ-ιδεῖν παρ-ιδών for inadvertent cases and ἀδίκημα / πλημμελεία (plēmmelia, "error / fault") for ma'al. The LXX's πλημμελεία and πλημμελέω are the standard Greek renderings of asham and ma'al in Leviticus, covering both the guilt/error and the guilt offering.

16. **v.15 — Asham / "guilt offering":** אָשָׁם (asham) in vv.14-19 shifts from the general sense of "guilt / reparation" (used in vv.1-13) to the specific designation of a distinct sacrifice: the guilt offering (asham). This is the formal introduction of the asham as a named offering type — it will receive fuller elaboration in Lev 7:1-7 (the priest's law for the asham). The asham differs from the chattat in two structural respects: (1) it specifies a ram (ayil), not a goat or bird, as the animal; (2) it requires monetary restitution (shillem) plus a one-fifth surcharge (chamishitho) in cases of misappropriation of sacred property. The asham thus combines sacrifice with economic reparation — a unique feature of this offering type. The LXX consistently renders asham as πλημμελεία (plēmmelia, "deviation / fault / guilt offering"), preserving the offense-and-offering double meaning.

17. **v.15 — Be-erkekha / "assessed by you" and shekel ha-qodesh / "shekel of the sanctuary":** בְּעֶרְכְּךָ (be-erkekha, "in your valuation / assessed by you") appears twice in v.15, addressed to Moses (and by extension the priest). The priest is to assess the monetary value of the ram and of the trespass. עֶרֶךְ (erekh, "value / assessment / valuation") is the key term of the vow/valuation legislation of Lev 27. Here it establishes that priestly authority is involved in monetary assessment — not a fixed price but a valuation made by the authoritative figure. The שֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ (shekel ha-qodesh, "shekel of the sanctuary") is the official, standardized weight used in cultic transactions — it may have been heavier than or equivalent to the commercial shekel; its designation "of the sanctuary" marks it as the benchmark of cultic integrity. The LXX renders ἐν τῷ ἀργυρίῳ τεταγμένῳ (en tō arguriō tetagmenō, "in the set / appointed silver"), using the language of regulation rather than transliterating shekel.

18. **v.16 — Chamishitho / "its fifth" or "one-fifth of it":** חֲמִישִׁתוֹ (chamishitho, "its fifth") is the twenty-percent surcharge added to the monetary restitution for trespass against sacred property. The construction is: (assessed value of misappropriated holy thing) + (one-fifth of that value) = total payment to the priest/sanctuary. This 20% penalty is paralleled in Lev 22:14 (eating sacred food by mistake), Lev 27:13, 15, 19, 27, 31 (redemption of dedicated things), and Num 5:7 (restitution of property wrongly withheld). The fifth-part surcharge functions as a deterrent and a signal that trespass against the LORD's things carries a financial consequence beyond mere replacement. The LXX renders τὸ ἐπίπεμπτον (to epipempton, "the added fifth"), a precise rendering.

19. **v.17 — "He did not know" (ve-lo yada):** The case of v.17 is the most theologically subtle in the chapter: a person has violated a divine prohibition without knowing it at the time, and here the condition "he did not know" remains in force — there is no additional phrase "and he comes to know it" (as in vv.3-4). This has generated significant interpretive debate. Does the person in v.17 discover the transgression afterward (as in vv.3-4), or is this a case where the person suspects guilt but cannot confirm it? The Talmudic tradition (Keritot 17b–26a) develops the concept of an "uncertain asham" (asham talui) based on this verse — the guilt offering brought when one is unsure whether a sin was committed. The phrase וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (ve-nasa avono, "he shall bear his iniquity") appears both here (v.17) and in v.1, creating a structural frame: v.1 (bearing guilt by concealing testimony) and v.17 (bearing guilt for unknown violation) are the two endpoints of the chapter's guilt spectrum — the deliberate and the entirely inadvertent.

20. **v.18 — Shgagato / "his error / his inadvertent act":** שְׁגָגָתוֹ (shgagato, "his inadvertent transgression / his error") is the noun form of bi-shgagah, the qualifier introduced at Lev 4:2 and used throughout chapters 4-5. The asham of v.18 is brought for the shgagah — the inadvertent, unintentional character of the act is confirmed again even at the point of atonement. This emphasis signals that the priestly system is not merely transactional (bring an offering and your record is cleared) but diagnostic: the offering addresses a specific moral and ritual condition — inadvertence, not defiance. The LXX renders ἀγνοίας αὐτοῦ (anoias autou, "his ignorance / his unknowing"), from agnoia, "ignorance," the standard Greek rendering for shgagah contexts.

21. **v.19 — Asham asham / "he has indeed incurred guilt":** The verse closes with the doubled formulation אָשֵׁם אָשָׁם (asham asham, "guilty he is guilty" or "he has indeed incurred guilt"), an emphatic infinitive absolute construction using the same root for both the noun and the participle. The first asham is the subject complement ("it is a guilt offering") and the second is the emphatic statement of the person's guilty status before the LORD. The verse is essentially a two-clause declaration: (1) הוּא אָשָׁם (hu asham, "it is a guilt offering") — identifying the sacrificial category; (2) אָשֹׁם אָשַׁם לַיהוָה (ashom asham la-YHWH, "he has surely incurred guilt before the LORD") — making the theological pronouncement. The doubled root is a rhetorical intensifier (cognate accusative / infinitive absolute) common in biblical Hebrew: "dying he shall die," "cursing he shall curse," etc. Here: "guilty he has been guilty." The LXX renders πλημμελῶν ἐπλημμέλησεν (plēmmeleōn eplēmmelēsen, "sinning he has sinned"), preserving the emphatic doubled construction.

---

### Structural Notes

Leviticus 5:1-19 falls into two distinct halves, each with its own sacrificial logic:

**Section A — The Sin Offering (Chattat) for Four Cases (vv.1-13):**

Four specific situations in which a person incurs guilt (asham) are enumerated:

| Verse | Case | Condition | Discovery | Offering |
|-------|------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Failing to testify under oath | Hears public oath-adjuration but withholds knowledge | No discovery clause | Guilt stated; offering resolution in v.6 |
| 2-3 | Touching animal or human uncleanness | Inadvertent contact with tum'ah | "Hidden, then comes to know" | Chattat (vv.6-13) |
| 4 | Rash oath | Swears rashly to do good or evil | "Hidden, then comes to know" | Chattat (vv.6-13) |

The atonement provision is then presented in three graduated tiers (vv.6-13):
- **Tier 1 (v.6):** Female lamb or female goat (chattat)
- **Tier 2 (vv.7-10):** Two birds — one chattat, one olah (when lamb/goat unaffordable)
- **Tier 3 (vv.11-13):** One-tenth ephah of plain flour (when even two birds unaffordable)

The graduated scale ensures that every Israelite, at every economic level, has access to atonement. The inclusion of a flour option is unique to chapter 5 among all the Levitical offering types, making the sin offering the most socially inclusive of all.

**Section B — The Guilt Offering (Asham) for Two Cases (vv.14-19):**

| Verse | Case | Animal | Additional Requirement |
|-------|------|--------|----------------------|
| 15-16 | Unintentional trespass against holy things | Ram (assessed value, shekel of sanctuary) | Restitution + one-fifth surcharge to priest |
| 17-19 | Unknowing violation of commandment | Ram (assessed value) | No restitution (no identifiable misappropriation) |

The asham of Section B differs structurally from the chattat of Section A:
1. **Specificity of animal:** The asham always requires a ram (ayil), not a graduated scale — there is no bird or flour option for the guilt offering.
2. **Monetary assessment:** The ram's value is set by priestly valuation (be-erkekha), and in the case of trespass against holy things, additional monetary restitution is required.
3. **Restitution principle:** The chattat addresses ritual defilement and guilt; the asham addresses a breach of what belongs to the LORD, combining sacrifice with economic repair. The person must both bring the animal and restore (plus 20%) what was misappropriated.

The chapter thus advances from Leviticus 4's four-case sin offering (structured by the rank of the offender) to a structure organized by the nature of the offense:
- Leviticus 4: Sin offering differentiated by **who sinned** (priest → congregation → leader → common person)
- Leviticus 5: Offerings differentiated by **what was done** (failure to testify; touching uncleanness; rash oath; trespass against holy things; unknowing violation)

The unifying thread is the root asham — guilt — which appears as verb (ashem, "to incur guilt"), noun (asham, "guilt offering"), and abstract (ashamah, "guilt reparation") across all 19 verses.

---

### Vaticanus LXX Comparison Summary (pp. 89–90)

The Vaticanus pages (pa_0089–pa_0090) present the LXX text of Leviticus 5 in continuous Greek uncials across three columns. The chapter header "CAP. XXXIII" is visible at the top of page 89/90, reflecting the LXX's chapter enumeration (LXX Lev 5 = MT Lev 5, though LXX sometimes numbers sections differently in the margin). The text is written in the characteristic Vaticanus hand — large, clear uncials with minimal word division and no breathings or accents, consistent with the 4th-century date.

Key LXX observations for Leviticus 5:

- **πλημμελεία / πλημμελέω (plēmmelia / plēmmelēo):** The LXX's characteristic rendering of asham throughout chapter 5. Πλημμελεία means "a false note / a discordant sound / an error / a fault," from the musical metaphor of a wrong note (para + melos, "beside the melody"). This is a striking choice for asham: the guilty person has struck a dissonant note in the harmony of Israel's covenant relationship with the LORD. The LXX's translation family thus casts guilt as a kind of moral discord — theologically resonant, though quite different from the Hebrew root's emphasis on liability and reparation.

- **ψυχή (psuche) for nefesh:** As throughout Leviticus, the LXX renders נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh) as ψυχή (psuche, "soul / life / person") — preserving the Hebrew's whole-person reference. The Vaticanus text reads ψυχή ἐὰν ἁμάρτῃ ("if a soul/person sins") at the case introductions.

- **ἀκούσιος / ἀγνοία (akousios / agnoia):** Both "involuntary" and "ignorance" vocabulary are deployed in the chapter for the various inadvertence conditions. The LXX distinguishes between the cases of vv.2-4 (where discovery after the fact is specified) and v.17 (where ignorance is the persistent condition), using agnoia more prominently in v.17-18, consistent with the MT's ve-lo yada.

- **μνημόσυνον (mnēmosunon) for azkarah:** The LXX's "memorial / remembrance" for azkarah at v.12 is consistent with its usage throughout the grain offering legislation (Lev 2:2, 9, 16; 6:15). The Vaticanus text's rendering reflects the same Vorlage as the MT — the azkarah is the handful burned as a "remembrance" before the LORD.

- **τὸ ἐπίπεμπτον (to epipempton) for chamishitho:** The "added fifth" (one-fifth surcharge) at v.16 is rendered with mathematical precision in the LXX. The preposition epi- ("upon / added to") combined with pempton ("fifth") gives "the fifth added on top" — a clear rendering of the surcharge logic.

- **MT/LXX alignment:** For Leviticus 5, the Vaticanus LXX and the MT are in very close agreement. There are no major expansions, omissions, or transpositions visible in the Vaticanus text for this chapter. The LXX's vocabulary choices (plēmmelia, akousios, psuche, mnēmosunon) are consistent throughout and represent a coherent, systematic translation policy rather than verse-by-verse variation.

— EMET
